title: Tomcat Configuration
breadcrumbs: Deployment Considerations for Performance and Scalability
text: Tomcat Configuration Configuring Tomcat properly can result in significant performance improvements. Use the following general guidelines: Separate Web servers and servlet containers. Some servlet containers, such as Tomcat, can service both servlet requests and Web requests. However, it is preferable to use a dedicated Web server, and a dedicated servlet container. Tomcat provides plug-ins to allow it to work with other Web servers, such as Apache and IIS. Use multiple instances of Tomcat when there are multiple processors. For example, a server might run one Apache server and three Tomcat servers. For more information, see Load Balancing with Tomcat . Configure the JK plug-in to balance loads among Tomcat instances by modifying the mod-jk.conf file. Tune Tomcat by editing the server.xml as follows: Set Reloadable=false for each context. Otherwise, Tomcat checks for changes in classes, creating unnecessary processing overhead. Set liveDeploy=false for the host. This setting creates a background thread to check for new Web application directories. Set debug=0 to prevent unnecessary logging Set minProcessors= Average number of concurrent requests Set maxProcessors= Twice the max number of concurrent requests Set acceptCount= 10 to limit the number of requests that can be accepted. Do not set the number of accepted requests too high. It is better to deny requests than accept too many pending requests, thereby negatively affecting all requests Set enableLookups=false to prevent DNS lookups, which can add significant delays Was this topic helpful?
